subject: UX & Research
title: Contextual Inquiry
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Contextual Inquiry.

hook.text: Instead of bringing users to a lab, go to them. Watch nurses use the medical system at the hospital. See the real mess.

buildup.text: Beyer and Holtzblatt created contextual inquiry in 1997. Research the user in their natural environment.

discovery.text: In labs, users perform artificial tasks cleanly. In context, you see interruptions, workarounds, and real chaos.

twist.text: A nurse's sticky notes on the monitor reveal that the system's reminder feature doesn't work. No survey catches that.

climax.text: Contextual inquiry is expensive but reveals problems invisible in any other method. Go where the work happens.

punchline.text: Labs are clean. Real life is messy. Research where users actually work.

quiz.question: Why is contextual inquiry more revealing than lab-based testing?
options: It captures real-world interruptions, workarounds, and environmental factors labs miss
It's cheaper than running a usability lab
Users perform better in familiar environments
